title: Africa
subtitle: Bronze, stone, cloth, and mud — craft from across the continent.
intro: Africa's art tradition is vast and old. These pieces range from 9th-century Igbo bronzes to the largest mud-brick building in the world.

works.title: Bronze Head from Ife
works.meta: Yoruba · 12th–15th c. · Nigeria
works.desc: Naturalistic portraits cast in brass with lost-wax technique. When first seen in Europe they were disbelieved as African work.

works.title: Benin Bronzes
works.meta: Edo kingdom · 16th–17th c. · Nigeria
works.desc: Plaques that once covered pillars of the royal palace in Benin City. Looted in 1897; still being repatriated today.

works.title: Bronze Head of Queen Idia
works.meta: Benin kingdom · 16th c. · Nigeria
works.desc: Portrait of the first Queen Mother of Benin, cast to sit on an ancestral altar. One of the most famous of the Benin bronzes.

works.title: Igbo-Ukwu Bronze
works.meta: Igbo · 9th c. · Nigeria
works.desc: Lost-wax castings of startling delicacy — three centuries before Ife, and predating most European bronzework of the same technical sophistication.

works.title: Great Zimbabwe
works.meta: Shona · 11th–15th c. · Zimbabwe
works.desc: Dry-stone walls built without mortar, enclosing a trade capital that controlled the gold road to the Indian Ocean.

works.title: Great Mosque of Kilwa
works.meta: Swahili coast · 11th–14th c. · Tanzania
works.desc: Coral-stone mosque on an island that dominated Indian Ocean trade. Ibn Battuta called Kilwa "one of the most beautiful cities in the world."

works.title: Rock-Hewn Churches of Lalibela
works.meta: Zagwe dynasty · 12th–13th c. · Ethiopia
works.desc: Eleven churches carved downward into solid rock. Meant as a new Jerusalem after pilgrimage routes were cut off.

works.title: Great Mosque of Djenné
works.meta: Mali · rebuilt 1907 (earlier mosque 13th c.)
works.desc: The world's largest mud-brick building. Every spring the whole town replasters it by hand in a single day.

works.title: Dogon Cliff Villages
works.meta: Dogon · 14th c. onward · Mali
works.desc: Mud granaries and shrines stacked along the Bandiagara Escarpment. The Dogon moved here fleeing forced conversion and stayed half a millennium.

works.title: Kente Cloth
works.meta: Asante & Ewe · 17th c. onward · Ghana
works.desc: Hand-woven silk and cotton strips sewn together into royal cloth. Each pattern has a name and a proverb behind it.

works.title: Obelisks of Aksum
works.meta: Aksumite kingdom · 4th c. · Ethiopia
works.desc: Granite stelae marking royal graves; the tallest once stood nearly a hundred feet high. One was looted by Mussolini, returned in 2005.

works.title: Nkisi Power Figure
works.meta: Kongo · 19th–20th c. · Central Africa
works.desc: Wooden figures bristling with nails and blades. Each nail driven in activated a pledge, a curse, or a contract witnessed by the spirit inside.

works.title: Senufo Sculpture
works.meta: Senufo · West Africa
works.desc: Elongated female figures and carved divination stools used by the Poro and Sandogo societies in Côte d'Ivoire and Mali.

works.title: Fang Reliquary (Byeri)
works.meta: Fang · 19th–20th c. · Gabon / Cameroon
works.desc: Carved guardian heads placed atop boxes of ancestral bones. Picasso saw one in 1907 and the Demoiselles d'Avignon followed.

works.title: Akan Goldweights
works.meta: Asante / Akan · 15th–19th c. · Ghana
works.desc: Brass figurines used to weigh gold dust. Each one is also a proverb — the weight you balance against is a riddle.

works.title: Lydenburg Heads
works.meta: Early Iron Age · 500 CE · South Africa
works.desc: Terracotta heads found buried under a farm in Mpumalanga — the earliest known African figurative sculpture south of the equator.

works.title: San Rock Art
works.meta: San · up to 30,000 years old · Southern Africa
works.desc: Eland, hunters, and trance figures painted on rock shelters from Namibia to the Drakensberg. One of humanity's longest unbroken painting traditions.

works.title: Golden Stool of the Asante
works.meta: Asante · 18th c. · Ghana
works.desc: Sacred seat that is said to contain the soul of the Asante nation. No one — not even the king — sits on it. A British demand to do so in 1900 sparked war.

works.title: Ibeji Twin Figures
works.meta: Yoruba · 19th c. onward · Nigeria
works.desc: When a twin dies, a carved figure stands in — fed, dressed, and cared for like the living. The Yoruba have one of the highest twinning rates in the world.

works.title: Larabanga Mosque
works.meta: Sudano-Sahelian · 15th c. · Ghana
works.desc: A mud-and-stick mosque painted white, bristling with wooden supports. The oldest mosque in West Africa still in its original form.

works.title: Queen Mother Pendant Mask (Iyoba)
works.meta: Edo kingdom · 16th c. · Nigeria
works.desc: Ivory mask worn on the belt of the Oba of Benin. The forehead tiaras are miniature Portuguese heads — trading partners carved as ornament.

works.title: Nok Terracotta
works.meta: Nok culture · 500 BCE – 500 CE · Nigeria
works.desc: Hollow terracotta heads with triangular eyes and pierced pupils. The oldest known figurative sculpture in sub-Saharan Africa.

works.title: Mapungubwe Hill
works.meta: Mapungubwe Kingdom · 1075–1220 · South Africa
works.desc: A sandstone hill-top capital that controlled Indian Ocean gold and ivory trade centuries before Great Zimbabwe. A golden rhinoceros was dug up from a royal grave here.

works.title: Ndop — Kuba King Portrait
works.meta: Kuba Kingdom · 17th c. onward · DR Congo
works.desc: Wooden royal portraits showing each Kuba king with a personal emblem — a drum, an anvil, a game board. Stand-ins who keep the king present after death.

works.title: Baule Sculpture
works.meta: Baule · 17th c. onward · Côte d'Ivoire
works.desc: Delicate wooden figures of "spirit spouses" from a parallel world, and calm portrait masks carved for Goli festivals. Among the most influential African sculptural traditions.

works.title: Asante Jewelry
works.meta: Akan / Asante · 19th c. · Ghana
works.desc: Gold jewelry for the chief and his court — beads, pectorals, rings cast with the same lost-wax technique as the goldweights. Sumptuary law regulated who could wear what.

works.title: Ethiopian Processional Cross
works.meta: Ethiopian Orthodox · 12th c. onward · Ethiopia
works.desc: Hand-held metal crosses carried at the front of church processions. Each is unique — geometries nested inside geometries, hammered out by a parish smith.

works.title: Brandberg Rock Paintings
works.meta: San · up to 2,000 years old · Namibia
works.desc: The Brandberg Massif hides thousands of rock paintings, most famously the "White Lady" — not a lady at all, but a medicine figure. Painted for ritual, not ornament.

works.title: Twyfelfontein Petroglyphs
works.meta: Pre-San hunter-gatherers · 6,000 years old · Namibia
works.desc: Thousands of rock engravings of giraffes, rhinos, and lions on sandstone. A seal, 900 km from the nearest sea, suggests long-distance travel — or long memory.

works.title: Red Bird
works.meta: Contemporary · c. 2000s · United Kingdom / Nigeria
works.desc: Chris Ofili's paintings layer resin-coated elephant dung onto glittering collage. His work interrogates Black identity and the politics of representation in Britain.

works.title: Shona Stone Sculpture
works.meta: Shona · 20th c. onward · Zimbabwe
works.desc: A post-colonial sculptural tradition carved from serpentine and springstone. Figures of spirits and ancestors, often semi-abstracted — now Zimbabwe's most recognized art export.

works.title: Adinkra Symbols
works.meta: Akan / Asante · 18th c. onward · Ghana
works.desc: A system of visual proverbs stamped or woven into cloth — each glyph carrying a name and an aphorism. Worn at funerals; later embraced globally as a Pan-African vocabulary.

works.title: Great Mosque of Kano
works.meta: Hausa · 15th c. onward · Nigeria
works.desc: A mud-and-stick Friday mosque rebuilt many times across six centuries. The current building rose after a 1937 fire; minarets slender as reeds above a walled city.

works.title: Kuba Appliqué Textiles
works.meta: Kuba Kingdom · 17th c. onward · DR Congo
works.desc: Raffia cloth embroidered with cut-pile geometrics. Men weave the base; women embroider. No two patterns repeat — each is a one-time composition.

works.title: Tuareg Metalwork
works.meta: Tuareg · 18th c. onward · Sahara
works.desc: Silver crosses, amulet cases, saddle fittings. Each Tuareg town has a signature cross shape; the Agadez cross is the most widely reproduced.

works.title: Akuaba
works.meta: Akan · traditional · Ghana
works.desc: Wooden fertility figures carried by women hoping to conceive. Small, abstracted, round-faced. Carved also now for collectors; the sacred ones are never sold.

works.title: Djinguereber Mosque
works.meta: Mali Empire · 1327 · Timbuktu
works.desc: Commissioned by Mansa Musa after his pilgrimage to Mecca. An Andalusian architect built it in earth and fossil limestone — one of three medieval mosques still standing in Timbuktu.

works.title: Sowei Helmet Mask
works.meta: Mende · 19th c. onward · Sierra Leone
works.desc: A black helmet mask worn in the Sande women's society initiation — the only African mask tradition danced exclusively by women, representing ideals of beauty and comportment.

works.title: Zulu Beadwork
works.meta: Zulu · 19th c. onward · South Africa
works.desc: Glass trade beads sewn onto leather and cotton in geometric patterns — colors and combinations encoding courtship messages ("love letters" in bead).

works.title: Fasil Ghebbi
works.meta: Ethiopian · 17th c. · Gondar
works.desc: A fortress-city of stone castles built by a succession of Solomonic emperors. The European-looking keeps are misleading — they're Ethiopian invention, not import.

works.title: Cairo Citadel
works.meta: Ayyubid → Ottoman · 1176 onward · Egypt
works.desc: Saladin's fortress above old Cairo, topped centuries later by the Mosque of Muhammad Ali with its tall twin minarets — a silhouette that defines the skyline.

works.title: Osun-Osogbo Sacred Grove
works.meta: Yoruba · 20th-c. revival of traditional · Nigeria
works.desc: A forest along the Oshun River filled with sculptures revived by the Austrian-Yoruba artist Susanne Wenger and local carvers — the last surviving intact sacred grove of the Yoruba.

works.title: Cape Coast Castle
works.meta: Colonial fortification · 1653 onward · Ghana
works.desc: A whitewashed castle on the Gulf of Guinea used for the Atlantic slave trade — enslaved Africans passed through its "Door of No Return." Now a museum, with whitewash still yearly.

works.title: Swahili Stone Towns
works.meta: Swahili coast · 12th–19th c. · East Africa
works.desc: Coral-stone trading towns — Lamu, Pate, Kilwa — with carved wooden doors studded in brass, Indian Ocean dhows in their harbors, and mosques whose minarets face inland.

works.title: Laas Geel Rock Paintings
works.meta: Neolithic pastoralist · c. 3500 BCE · Somaliland
works.desc: A complex of granite shelters filled with some of Africa's best-preserved rock art: cattle draped in ceremonial robes, their horns painted in alternating red and white, with small human figures raising arms beneath them. Only rediscovered by archaeologists in 2002.

works.title: Bandiagara Escarpment
works.meta: Tellem and Dogon · c. 1000 CE onward · Mali
works.desc: A 150-kilometer sandstone cliff that the Tellem used as burial shafts before the Dogon arrived in the fifteenth century. Granaries and shrines cling to the rockface like swallows' nests; the Dogon say their cosmology is written into the architecture.

works.title: Ikenga
works.meta: Igbo · pre-1900 · southeast Nigeria
works.desc: Personal shrines carved as horned male figures, each one owned by a man and activated through sacrifice to embody the 'right hand' — the strength that accomplishes things. When the owner dies the Ikenga is split in two.

works.title: Chiwara Headdress
works.meta: Bamana · 19th–20th century · Mali
works.desc: A roan antelope carved in openwork wood worn by young men who danced in pairs at planting season. The Bamana say Chiwara is the mythical being who first taught humans to farm, bent over the earth like the antelope's graceful curve.

works.title: Ndebele House Painting
works.meta: Ndebele · 20th century · South Africa
works.desc: Exterior walls painted freehand by women in sharp-edged chevrons, diamonds, and vivid blocks of color, applied without grid or ruler. The practice emerged after the 1883 Ndebele defeat as a coded language of resistance preserved inside everyday domestic space.

works.title: Kasubi Tombs
works.meta: Buganda kingdom · 1882 · Uganda
works.desc: The burial palace of four kabakas of Buganda, a vast thatched dome called Muzibu-Azaala-Mpanga supported by rings of wooden columns wrapped in bark cloth. Fire destroyed the main structure in 2010 and a painstaking reconstruction is ongoing.

works.title: Koutammakou (Takienta Tower-Houses)
works.meta: Batammariba · 17th century onward · Togo/Benin
works.desc: Fortified two-story mud towers with conical thatched granary turrets, built by the Batammariba on the Togo-Benin border. Each house is read as a human body — entrance mouth, granary head — and its construction ritualized every stage of a family's life.

works.title: Golden Rhinoceros of Mapungubwe
works.meta: Mapungubwe · c. 1220–1290 CE · South Africa
works.desc: A small wooden figurine plated with hammered gold foil, unearthed from a royal grave on Mapungubwe Hill in 1934. Apartheid-era archaeology hid the find for decades because it proved sophisticated African statehood before European contact.

works.title: Church of Saint George, Lalibela
works.meta: Zagwe dynasty · late 12th–early 13th century · Ethiopia
works.desc: Carved top-down into a single block of red volcanic tuff in the shape of a Greek cross, thirty meters on a side. King Lalibela commissioned eleven such monolithic churches after Jerusalem fell to Saladin, intending to build a new Holy Land in the Ethiopian highlands.

works.title: Yeha Great Temple
works.meta: D'mt kingdom · c. 700 BCE · Ethiopia
works.desc: A rectangular limestone sanctuary built without mortar, oriented to the rising of the moon god Almaqah. Its precise dry-stone masonry is the oldest standing building in Ethiopia and predates the Aksumite kings by nearly a millennium.

works.title: Debre Damo Monastery
works.meta: Aksumite · 6th century · Ethiopia
works.desc: Perched atop a flat-topped mountain reachable only by a 15-meter rope hauled up by the monks. Its interior preserves carved wooden coffers and rafters that are the oldest surviving examples of Aksumite architecture.

works.title: Harar Jugol
works.meta: Walled old city · 16th century · Ethiopia
works.desc: Called Islam's fourth holy city, with 82 mosques and 102 shrines packed inside its five-gated wall. Harari houses are painted bright interior hues and display hung baskets — the craft tradition of married women signaling wealth and taste.

works.title: Tsodilo Hills Rock Paintings
works.meta: San · c. 24,000 BCE–1800 CE · Botswana
works.desc: More than four thousand paintings on the quartzite outcrops the San call 'the Louvre of the desert.' Eland and giraffes predominate, but red-pigment handprints dating to the last Ice Age make this one of the longest continuously used sacred sites on earth.

works.title: Blombos Cave Engravings
works.meta: Middle Stone Age · c. 75,000 BCE · South Africa
works.desc: Deliberate crosshatch patterns incised on ochre and shell beads perforated with bone awls — among the earliest evidence of symbolic thought ever found. The cave pushed back the dawn of human art by tens of thousands of years.

works.title: Tomb of Askia
works.meta: Songhai Empire · 1495 · Gao, Mali
works.desc: A seventeen-meter pyramidal mud tower bristling with protruding palm-wood timbers, built for emperor Askia Mohammed after his pilgrimage to Mecca. The timbers double as permanent scaffolding for the annual replastering of its earthen skin.

works.title: Sankore Madrasa
works.meta: Mali/Songhai · c. 989 onward · Timbuktu
works.desc: An earthen mosque-university whose pyramidal minaret's dimensions match the Kaaba in Mecca. Its scholars curated one of the largest libraries in medieval Africa; tens of thousands of manuscripts from Sankore survive, hidden in family chests when Tuareg raiders closed in.

works.title: Khami Ruins
works.meta: Torwa state · c. 1450–1650 · Zimbabwe
works.desc: A stone city of terraced platforms retained by decorative chevron and checker patterns in dry-laid granite. Successor to Great Zimbabwe, Khami traded Ming porcelain and Portuguese beads with the east African coast before being burned in the seventeenth century.

works.title: Elmina Castle
works.meta: Portuguese colonial · 1482 · Ghana
works.desc: Whitewashed on a rock above the Gulf of Guinea — the oldest European building in sub-Saharan Africa. Started as a trading post for gold, became a holding point for the Atlantic slave trade.

works.title: Herero Dress
works.meta: Herero · late 19th c. onward · Namibia
works.desc: Floor-length Victorian gowns adopted from 19th-century German missionaries — kept, inverted, made defiantly Herero. The horned headdress honors cattle; the silhouette accuses a colonial past.

works.title: Great Mosque of Touba
works.meta: Mouride · 1963 · Senegal
works.desc: Founded by the Sufi saint Amadou Bamba; the current mosque completed in the 1960s. Five minarets and a green dome; a gathering of millions every year at the Magal pilgrimage.

works.title: Mbuti Bark Cloth
works.meta: Mbuti · traditional · Ituri Rainforest
works.desc: Beaten fig-bark cloth painted by women with dots, curves, and grids in charcoal, juice, and mud. Every panel is an individual composition — one of Africa's most abstract traditional visual idioms.

works.title: Timbuktu Manuscripts
works.meta: West African Islamic · 13th c. onward · Mali
works.desc: Hundreds of thousands of hand-copied manuscripts on astronomy, law, medicine, poetry. Families smuggled them out of Timbuktu in 2013 as jihadists approached, hiding them in cellars and metal chests.

works.title: Ethiopian Illuminated Bible
works.meta: Ethiopian Orthodox · 15th c. onward
works.desc: Ge'ez-language bibles painted on parchment with bold figural interior pages — a distinctly Ethiopian Christian visual tradition that ran continuously from late antiquity to the 20th century.

works.title: Apollo 11 Cave Stones
works.meta: Namibia · c. 25,000 BCE
works.desc: Seven palm-sized slabs painted in charcoal and ochre with therianthropic creatures — part-human, part-animal. Among the oldest figurative art anywhere in Africa.

works.title: Tassili n'Ajjer Rock Art
works.meta: Saharan prehistoric · 10th millennium BCE onward · Algeria
works.desc: Thousands of paintings and engravings high on a sandstone plateau — hunters, giraffes, cattle herds, swimmers. The Sahara when it was green.

works.title: Kanaga Mask
works.meta: Dogon · 19th–20th c. · Mali
works.desc: Tall crossbar mask worn at funerary dama dances. The double-plank form is read as sky-over-earth, God-above-man, or a bird in flight depending on the dancer.

works.title: Bwa Plank Mask
works.meta: Bwa · 20th c. · Burkina Faso
works.desc: Towering rectangular mask crowned by a crescent, carved and painted in the Bwa Bayiri style. Worn at agricultural rites; the concentric patterns are protective scarification translated into wood.

works.title: Kuosi Elephant Mask
works.meta: Bamileke · 19th–20th c. · Cameroon
works.desc: A hooded bead-and-cloth mask of the Kuosi secret society. The long panels hanging front and back are the elephant's trunk; wearing it, a Bamileke elite danced a living symbol of royal power.

works.title: Earth's Skin
works.meta: Contemporary · 2007 · El Anatsui
works.desc: A shimmering 'cloth' made of thousands of liquor-bottle caps wired together — draped like a kente cloth but on an architectural scale. Hangs differently every time it's installed.

works.title: Chi Wara Antelope Headdress
works.meta: Bamana · 19th–20th c. · Mali
works.desc: Carved wooden antelopes worn on the head during planting rituals, male and female dancing as a pair. Chi Wara is the spirit who taught humans to farm.

works.title: Kota Reliquary Figure
works.meta: Kota · 19th c. · Gabon
works.desc: A flat, copper-sheeted abstract face on a diamond-shaped body — the guardian of a basket of ancestral bones. The staring form entranced Picasso and the Cubists when it arrived in Paris.

works.title: Luba Lukasa
works.meta: Luba · 19th c. onward · DRC
works.desc: A wooden memory board studded with beads and pegs — a tactile mnemonic for court history, used by trained ritualists. No two lukasa are meant to be read exactly the same way.

works.title: Gedi Ruins
works.meta: Swahili · 12th–17th c. · Kenya
works.desc: A coral-stone town abandoned in the 17th century for reasons still debated — piracy, drought, Galla raids. Mosque, palace, houses with Chinese pottery fragments still in the walls.

works.title: Songye Nkishi Figure
works.meta: Songye · 19th–20th c. · DRC
works.desc: Bulky wooden figure bristling with nails, horns, brass tacks, beads — each addition a prayer, accusation, or cure. Kept by a community-appointed specialist, fed with offerings over generations.

works.title: Hemba Ancestor Figure
works.meta: Hemba · 19th–20th c. · DRC
works.desc: A standing male figure, hands on belly, face serene — a portrait of a clan ancestor rather than a generic spirit. The Hemba treat these statues as functional genealogies in wood.

works.title: Rock-Hewn Churches of Tigray
works.meta: Ethiopian Orthodox · 6th c. onward · Ethiopia
works.desc: Dozens of churches carved directly into cliff faces and mountains, some older than Lalibela, many reached only by rope or handhold climbs. Still in active use.

works.title: Sidibé Studio Portrait
works.meta: Modern photography · 1960s–70s · Mali
works.desc: Malick Sidibé shot Bamako's young for two decades — dancers at night parties, couples posed in matching bell-bottoms. A lens on independence-era West African youth at its most self-invented.

works.title: Senufo Rhythm Pounder
works.meta: Senufo · 19th–20th c. · Côte d'Ivoire
works.desc: A tall, slender standing figure beaten against the ground to mark the rhythm of funerary processions — the Deble. Its abraded feet testify to decades of use.

works.title: Pendant Mask of Iyoba Idia
works.meta: Edo / Benin Kingdom · 16th c. · Nigeria
works.desc: Ivory hip pendant worn at the Oba's waist — the queen mother's face framed by tiny carved Portuguese heads. Looted in 1897; a version is the emblem of FESTAC '77, Africa's mass Pan-African arts festival.

works.title: Gwandusu
works.meta: Bamana · 15th–17th c. · Mali
works.desc: A towering wooden mother-and-child figure — "extraordinary" is what the name means. Carved by a Bamana blacksmith for a men's association; the tallest, oldest intact Bamana figure known.

works.title: Ifá Diviner's Necklace
works.meta: Yoruba · 19th–20th c. · Nigeria
works.desc: A beaded necklace worn by babaláwos — priests of Ifá divination. Colors encode the 256 figures of the Odu corpus, the Yoruba system for reading a client's fate.

works.title: Yoruba Altar Figure
works.meta: Yoruba · 18th c. · Nigeria
works.desc: A copper-alloy onílé figure that stood on a shrine — "owner of the house" — tall, delicate, hands held up in salutation. Yoruba brass casting far from Benin's more famous tradition.

works.title: Yoruba Beaded Crown
works.meta: Yoruba · 19th–20th c. · Nigeria
works.desc: A conical bead-veiled crown (adéńlá) worn only by a dynastic king — the veil hides the face because the oba's power is too much to meet directly. Made of tens of thousands of imported glass beads.

works.title: Shrine Figure for Sàngó
works.meta: Yoruba · c. 1905 · Nigeria
works.desc: A carved wooden shrine figure for the thunder-god Sàngó — kneeling, holding a double-axe staff, head crowned with a wide Sàngó coif. Among the few Yoruba sculptures attributed to a named workshop.

works.title: Èṣù Dance Staff
works.meta: Yoruba · 19th c. · Nigeria
works.desc: A staff topped with a kneeling figure wearing a phallic topknot — Èṣù, the trickster-messenger who stands at every crossroads. Cowries dangle from the shaft; the staff was swung in ritual dance.

works.title: Ethiopian Gospel Leaf
works.meta: Ethiopian Orthodox · c. 1440–80
works.desc: A single illuminated leaf from a gospel book, Saint Luke portrait painted in tempera on vellum. Ethiopian manuscripts kept this painterly tradition alive continuously from late antiquity through the 20th century.

works.title: Dogon Satimbe Mask
works.meta: Dogon · early–mid 20th c. · Mali
works.desc: A tall wooden mask surmounted by a standing female figure with raised arms — the satimbe, "sister on the head." Worn by men to represent the mythic first woman who discovered the masks.

works.title: Senufo Nursing Woman
works.meta: Senufo · 18th–20th c. · Côte d'Ivoire
works.desc: A wooden mother-and-child figure, baby at her breast, body elongated and severe. Used in the Poro men's initiation society — the woman the boys are being taught to leave behind.

works.title: Akan Memorial Head (Nsodie)
works.meta: Akan · late 17th c. · Ghana
works.desc: A terracotta portrait head made at the death of a royal — abstracted features, shaved pattern on the crown. Akan nsodie were modeled by women potters, stood upright on graves, and left to weather back into earth.

works.title: Dan Face Mask
works.meta: Dan · early 20th c. · Liberia / Côte d'Ivoire
works.desc: A small polished mask — serene, almost hypnotically calm. The Dan made finely finished masks to house gle spirits summoned for judgments, entertainments, or wrestling matches.

works.title: Asante Gold Lion Ornament
works.meta: Asante · 19th c. · Ghana
works.desc: A small cast-gold lion worn by royal courtiers — the Asante king's gold-staff bearers, messengers, and swordbearers all carried miniatures that marked their role.

works.title: Ethiopian Mäsqälä Qǝddase
works.meta: Ethiopian Orthodox · 13th–14th c.
works.desc: A bronze hand cross with elaborate interlace — "cross of the Mass," carried by priests during the Qǝddase liturgy. Each Ethiopian cross design is an improvised variation on the theme.

works.title: Yäʿğğ Mäsqäl
works.meta: Ethiopian · 18th c. · Tigray
works.desc: A wood-and-metal hand cross used for personal blessing — the priest kisses it, presses it to the forehead of each worshipper. Lattices of ornamental crosses ring the central form.

works.title: Kongo Crucifix
works.meta: Kongo · late 19th c. · Central Africa
works.desc: A carved crucifix with Christ depicted with Kongo features and a flat, wide-nosed face — Christianity arrived with the Portuguese in 1491, and Kongo Christ became genuinely Kongo.

works.title: Kongo Prestige Cap (Mpu Ngola)
works.meta: Kongo · 19th c. · DRC
works.desc: A conical raffia cap crowned with glass beads and brass tacks, worn by chiefs and diviners. The "mpu" projected rank visible across the village square.

works.title: Kongo Power Figure (Nkisi)
works.meta: Kongo · late 19th c. · Central Africa
works.desc: A wooden figure accumulating nails, blades, and resin packets — each added by a ritual specialist to bind a specific prayer or curse. The object is a growing archive of contracts.

works.title: Agboho Mmuo Mask
works.meta: Igbo · early 20th c. · Nigeria
works.desc: A wooden "maiden spirit" mask, painted white with delicately slitted eyes and painted lips — the kind of face Igbo men wore to embody the spirits of beautiful young women.

works.title: Asante Knife-Sheath Tip (Sika Boha)
works.meta: Asante · 19th c. · Ghana
works.desc: A cast-gold terminal for a ceremonial knife sheath, the gold pounded and modeled into tiny emblematic forms — hidden in the court's regalia and worn only on state occasions.

works.title: Benin Commemorative Head (Uhunmwun-Elao)
works.meta: Benin Kingdom · 16th c. · Nigeria
works.desc: A brass head of a dead oba, intended for his ancestral altar — one of the earliest surviving Benin heads, smaller and less stylized than the later ones before looting.

works.title: Benin Rattle Staff (Ukhuhre)
works.meta: Benin Kingdom · early 20th c.
works.desc: A carved wooden staff shaken during altar rituals to summon ancestral spirits. Each court official had a rattle staff marking his office.

works.title: Chokwe Ceremonial Throne
works.meta: Chokwe · 19th c. · Angola
works.desc: A backed wooden chair (citwamo ca mangu) carved with figures standing, sitting, copulating along the arms and rungs — royal regalia adapted from European chairs seen via Portuguese trade.

works.title: Yoruba Gelede Mask
works.meta: Yoruba · early 20th c. · Nigeria
works.desc: A carved wooden mask for a Gelede festival — the Yoruba annual rite honoring mothers and elderly women, whose "witchcraft" powers had to be appeased by men dancing as women.

works.title: Lobi Baàthíl
works.meta: Lobi · c. 1900 · Burkina Faso
works.desc: A small stiff wooden ancestor figure (baàthíl) kept on a household shrine. Lobi figures guard against illness and misfortune — if they fail they are disciplined, buried, or burned.

works.title: Nesting Wedding Baskets
works.meta: Western Sudan · c. 1900 · Burkina Faso region
works.desc: A set of ten coiled baskets nesting into one another, each smaller than the last — part of a bride's dowry, filling a single large vessel that would be opened at the husband's family house.

works.title: Kuba Bwoom Mask
works.meta: Kuba · Congo · mid–late 19th c. · wood and paint
works.desc: An initiation helmet mask with bulging forehead — Bwoom represents a commoner or prince opposite two royal masks; at masquerades the three perform a mythic family argument.

works.title: Yombe Phemba Mother and Child
works.meta: Yombe · Congo · mid–late 19th c. · wood
works.desc: A seated Yombe mother with her child — phemba figures held fertility medicine in a cavity in the back, carried by women struggling to conceive.

works.title: Mangbetu Figurative Harp (Domu)
works.meta: Mangbetu · Congo · early 20th c. · wood and rawhide
works.desc: An arched harp topped with a female head in elongated Mangbetu style — head-binding was a real beauty practice, and musicians in the 1920s Ituri played these at King Okondo's palace.

works.title: Baga Serpent Headdress
works.meta: Baga · Guinea · late 19th–early 20th c. · wood and paint
works.desc: A tall painted wooden serpent worn above the dancer's head. When colonial missions banned the Baga cult in the 1950s the masks burned or sold abroad; the form was revived in 1985.

works.title: Bamileke Elephant Mask (Mbap Mteng)
works.meta: Bamileke · Cameroon Grassfields · early 20th c. · beads on cloth
works.desc: A beaded cloth elephant mask with long trunk panels — worn by members of the Kuosi society, it declared the wearer's wealth through thousands of imported glass beads.

works.title: Sapi Nomoli Figure
works.meta: Sapi · Sierra Leone · 15th c. · soapstone
works.desc: A soapstone seated figure carved by the Sapi before Portuguese contact — later farmers pulled these up from their fields, called them nomoli, and placed them in rice shrines as house-spirits.

works.title: Djenné-Djenno Terracotta Figure
works.meta: Djenné-Djenno · Mali · 14th–17th c. · terracotta
works.desc: A terracotta figure from sub-Saharan Africa's oldest city, found buried in abandoned house-floors. Most were looted in the 1970s–80s; provenance is now a permanent scandal of the trade.

works.title: Lega Bwami Iginga Figure
works.meta: Lega · Congo · 19th c. · elephant ivory
works.desc: A small ivory figurine used in the Bwami initiation — each iginga embodied a proverb the initiate had to memorize. Bwami is an ethical order: the higher the rank, the more elaborate the ivory.
